source.title: Outcome of first-episode schizophrenia in India: longitudinal study of effect of insight and psychopathology

confidence_rationale: Prospective 1-year cohort study (n=131 first-episode schizophrenia patients in Vellore, India; n=115 with follow-up data) showing that DUP and changes in insight during the first 6 months strongly predicted outcome (relapse / functional impairment), controlling for baseline measures. Anthony David is senior author (developer of the SAI insight scale). Source quality is high. Mapping fidelity is the issue: DUP is a proxy for cumulative commit-boundary exposure under the delusion, but it also covaries with severity, comorbid use, social stigma, and access barriers; the paper does not separate enacted from non-enacted delusional content. So this supports the prediction that earlier intervention is better but does not isolate the proposed mechanism from prior-strength accounts.
failure_signatures: DUP correlates with many things besides number of typed commit-boundary crossings; the predictive relationship between DUP and insight trajectory is consistent with the proposed mechanism but also with severity-only accounts and with prior-strength accounts. The study design cannot distinguish.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In first-episode schizophrenia, both duration of untreated psychosis and changes in insight during the first six months strongly and independently predict relapse and functional impairment at 1 year, controlling for baseline psychopathology.
mapping.ree_translation: Consistent with the prediction that exposure to commit-boundary crossings under the delusional belief (proxied by DUP) increases the cost of subsequent belief revision (proxied by insight trajectory). The prediction the brief makes is more specific than what the paper tests: the proposed mechanism predicts that enacted delusional content should be harder to revise than non-enacted delusional content of matched severity, and DUP does not separate these.
mapping.mapping_caveat: DUP is a non-specific proxy. Patients with longer DUP differ in many ways from those with shorter DUP that have nothing to do with the commit-boundary lock mechanism (severity, social support, access to care, stigma, comorbid substance use). A direct test would require comparing insight trajectories for patients matched on DUP and severity but differing in the extent to which the delusion has driven specific committed actions. No such study exists in the cohort literature I could find.
mapping.source_context: First-episode schizophrenia patients; Vellore, India; 1-year prospective follow-up; SAI for insight; standard psychopathology measures.

confidence_components.notes: BJP, well-conducted cohort, senior author is the SAI developer. Mapping fidelity is the limiting factor — DUP is the closest proxy I can find for accumulated commit-boundary exposure but it is a weak proxy. Transfer risk is the cross-cultural generalization (Indian cohort) but the insight-DUP relationship has been replicated in Western samples.
